content:
{
  "title": "Dynamic Counter-Stereotype Prompting for Bias Mitigation in LLMs",
  "problem": "Large language models (LLMs) often reproduce social biases and stereotypes in generated text, even when prompts are neutral or intended to be factual. Prompting methods to reduce such biases remain limited in both robustness and generalizability.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are increasingly integrated into decision-support and user-facing applications, mitigating their propagation of social biases becomes critical. Existing fine-tuning and static prompting methods are either costly or limited in scope. A lightweight, prompt-based intervention that generalizes across contexts is urgently needed.",
  "method": "We propose Dynamic Counter-Stereotype Prompting (DCP): for each input, an auxiliary instruction is generated in real-time, requesting the model to explicitly avoid common stereotypes and encourage alternative perspectives. This auxiliary instruction is dynamically conditioned on the detected topic (e.g., gender, race, occupation) using a lightweight topic classifier. The final prompt concatenates the user’s original input with the tailored counter-stereotype directive, aiming to prime the model away from default biases without extensive fine-tuning.",
  "experiment_plan": "We test DCP using the StereoSet and CrowS-Pairs datasets, comparing bias scores and language quality against vanilla and generic debiasing prompts. Metrics include stereotype score reduction, task relevance (accuracy/fluency), and human evaluation of bias. A/B testing with and without dynamic tailoring will assess DCP's effectiveness and generalizability."
}
